title: Paddyï؟½s Letter, 1857
content:
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve had all sorts of luck, sometimes bad, sometimes better,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ But now I have somebodyï؟½s luck and my own,
آ آ آ آ For I stooped in the street and I picked up a letter,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Which some one had written to send away home.

آ آ آ آ The old adage says, ï؟½What you find, you may keep it,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ And as most of these old sayings are very true,
آ آ آ آ I straight broke the seal, and then having read it,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ The contents of this letter I tell unto you.

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ  The Letter

آ آ آ آ Dear Dermot, I hope when this letter gets to you
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twill find you in health, as now it leaves me;
آ آ آ آ But I hope youï؟½re more happy than I am in Australiaï؟½
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ If not, itï؟½s small comfort that you have, achree!

آ آ آ آ Hard fortuneï؟½s been mine since crossing the line,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Though that same I neï؟½er saw, for we crossed it at night;
آ آ آ آ But they say ï؟½twas laid down at expense of the Crown,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ To divide the wrong side of the world from the right.

آ آ آ آ But what should a boy placed in my situation
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Know about lines laid across the big sea!
آ آ آ آ But, faith, this I know, and without navigation,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Iï؟½m at the wrong side of the line, anyway.

آ آ آ آ Iï؟½m telling you now how strange seasons fall.
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ We have here rain and sleet in the month of July,
آ آ آ آ And hailstones as big as a small cannon-ballï؟½
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ And they do as much harmï؟½not a word of a lie!

آ آ آ آ But the making of magistrates now all the rage is,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ And every flockmasterï؟½s a justice of peace;
آ آ آ آ They find it so easy to cancel the wages,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ The law is their own and they rob whom they please.

آ آ آ آ Pat Murphyï؟½s boy Tim, that married Moll Casey,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Lives on the Barcoo thatï؟½s away in the bush.
آ آ آ آ Himself and the wife, why they lived mighty aisy,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Till one day on Tim, oh, the blacks they did rush.

آ آ آ آ They killed little Paddy, but spared the young baby,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Because it was sicklyï؟½I think it was thatï؟½
آ آ آ آ And while Molly was crying, a gin said, ï؟½No habbie
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Your thin picaninnyï؟½well wait till itï؟½s fat.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis a beautiful country to practise economy.
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Though the houses out here are not quite waterproof,
آ آ آ آ But theyï؟½re illigant houses for studying astronomyï؟½
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ You can lie on your back and read stars through the roof

آ آ آ آ P.S.ï؟½This is crampedï؟½if thereï؟½s no one to read it,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Send for Tim Murphy, heï؟½ll know every stroke.
آ آ آ آ Ye all have my blessing, I know that yell need it,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ So no more at present from Teddy Oï؟½Rourke.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
